title: My Favorite Moment
wikitext:
[]: My Favorite Moment             Quote Post by badman89 » Mon Jan 17, 2011 1:00 pm Aesthetically speaking, my favorite moment of the entire series occurs at the end of episode 26 - it's the only time during the show that I actually felt like crying. (I’m talking about the TV show, not the movie). Towards the end of episode 26 Shinji, within the framework of the Human Instrumentality Project, constructs a world in which he is a normal fourteen year old boy. We are presented with something like Shinji's ideal version of his life: both of his parents are well alive and happily married; Asuka is Shinji's classmate and close childhood friend and is sort of like a big-sister in his life; Rei is a new student in their class and a possible romantic interest for Shinji; Misato is Shinji's class teacher and as for the Evas, angels and all the other dark elements of Shinji's life in Tokyo 3 there is no trace of them. The world is a bright and happy place. We also find resonance of this atmosphere in the music - it's the same music that we get during the most light-hearted moments of the show such as Shinji, Misato and Pen Pen having breakfast. I often wonder what merit this scene has and how it can have the power to make me want to cry. Considered on it's own it's a rather silly scene. It's the kind of scene I would expect to find in a high school comedy genre show - the kind of show that I would never ever watch. So why is this scene so powerful here? I don't at all have a proper answer to this question (this is indeed what I'm looking for here). All I am able to do is make two points towards answering this question in the hope of generating some further fruitful discussion on the topic: 1. This scene derives its power from the context within which it occurs. Everything we have learned about the world of Shinji and Tokyo-3 from the preceding twenty five and a half episodes is very very dark. Overall this show is dark, depressing and emotionally burdensome. Watching this show is like carrying a heavy emotional rock on your shoulders but suddenly in this one scene it is as if the rock has been lifted. This results in a feeling of lightness and “traditional” happiness that has a mystifying power over the viewer. I am mystified that the darkness can be cleared so easily – but I realize at once that this is a false clearing. This bright and happy world that we are presented with is not the real world but it is just a small speck in the vast abyss that is the Human Instrumentality Project. The bright and happy world is probably not even real, just a fragment of Shinji’s imagination (but of course, in the HIP it is difficult to say what is real and what isn’t – but in any case this particular happy world of Shinji’s is just one possibility among millions in terms of the HIP). But in spite of the falsity of this world, I find that I want to cling on to it more than anything in the world. 2. This brings me to the second point - this scene plays heavily with the theme of expectation vs. realization in the show. Why is it that based on what we have seen we expect things in the show to turn out a certain way? For example, why does a large part of the audience expect the show to end in a certain way? And in what way does the show's actual ending realize / not realize this expectation. Actually, this false scene that I’m talking about is probably the ending that everybody secretly hoped for. We all wanted everything to turn out ok for Shinji and his world. (We must ask here why we hoped for this?) And the show delivers this to us – it delivers what we wanted and hoped but it delivers it in a twisted and ironic fashion. It is almost as if the show is teasing the viewer by dangling this possibility in front of him, showing him the ending that he always wanted but simultaneously destroying the basis for this hope. The hope is that none of the bad stuff ever happened (angels, evas, human instrumentality, etc.) and that instead Shinji lives a normal life that is “happy.” This is exactly what is presented to us in the scene in question; but what is behind this presentation? In fact, it’s just all the bad stuff. That’s what is the reality now – all that bad stuff. So in fact, it’s not really the ending that we were hoping for, it just has the appearance of the ending that we were hoping for. But why is this appearance so powerful? It seems to satisfy some desire that the viewer has. But how can it satisfy this desire while at the same time undercutting the entire basis of that desire? ---- As a final note, when I was watching this scene for the first time, I seriously thought that the end credits for the show would roll on in this scene i.e. I thought that this was the closing scene of the TV show but it isn’t. Shinji then goes on to recognize that what we just saw was merely one possibility amongst many others in the HIP and he finally understands what HIP is all about (and everyone applauds his realization). I personally think that the TV show should have ended with this scene in Shinji’s imaginiary life where everything is “ calm, peaceful and tranquil” (as is said by Toji’s friend, Kensuke). That would have been a beautiful ending to a beautiful show in my opinion.

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Mon Jan 17, 2011 1:39 pm But if it ended there, the theme would be lost. The idea of not running from reality is.... Ugh. I can't put what I want to say into words. Can anyone help with that?

[]:             Quote Post by Azathoth » Mon Jan 17, 2011 1:40 pm TehDonutKing wrote: Ugh. I can't put what I want to say into words. Can anyone help with that?

[]: Re: My Favorite Moment             Quote Post by die-yng » Mon Jan 17, 2011 3:23 pm badman89 wrote: 2. This brings me to the second point - this scene plays heavily with the theme of expectation vs. realization in the show. Why is it that based on what we have seen we expect things in the show to turn out a certain way? For example, why does a large part of the audience expect the show to end in a certain way? And in what way does the show's actual ending realize / not realize this expectation. Actually, this false scene that I’m talking about is probably the ending that everybody secretly hoped for. We all wanted everything to turn out ok for Shinji and his world. (We must ask here why we hoped for this?) And the show delivers this to us – it delivers what we wanted and hoped but it delivers it in a twisted and ironic fashion. It is almost as if the show is teasing the viewer by dangling this possibility in front of him, showing him the ending that he always wanted but simultaneously destroying the basis for this hope. The hope is that none of the bad stuff ever happened (angels, evas, human instrumentality, etc.) and that instead Shinji lives a normal life that is “happy.” This is exactly what is presented to us in the scene in question; but what is behind this presentation? In fact, it’s just all the bad stuff. That’s what is the reality now – all that bad stuff. So in fact, it’s not really the ending that we were hoping for, it just has the appearance of the ending that we were hoping for. But why is this appearance so powerful? It seems to satisfy some desire that the viewer has. But how can it satisfy this desire while at the same time undercutting the entire basis of that desire?

[]:             Quote Post by Jornophelanthas » Mon Jan 17, 2011 5:04 pm TehDonutKing wrote: But if it ended there, the theme would be lost. The idea of not running from reality is.... Ugh. I can't put what I want to say into words. Can anyone help with that?

[]:             Quote Post by Trajan » Mon Jan 17, 2011 5:58 pm I always thought the whole “School / Congratulations” scene was just a hallucination or dream brought on by Third Impact where Shinji idealizes the perfect life for himself and imagines that everybody loves him so he doesn't have to run away anymore. I don’t think he’s accepting reality, I think he’s still running away from it.

[]:             Quote Post by Sailor Star Dust » Mon Jan 17, 2011 7:59 pm For visual references (and without bringing up concurrency too deeply >_>), the auditorium blows away/explodes in a similar manner of the Black Moon exploding in EoE... And both sequences are followed up or preceding "life-affirming" images of cheerful cast members against a blue sky with clouds (26: "Congratulations!", EoE: Episode 15 Group Photo "I want to see them again because my feelings were real."). Also, I thought the Possible World of Reality in 26 (later the GoS2 game and manga) was a parody of cliche romance-comedy anime what with the perverted lead, childhood friend, transfer student... The After the End audio-drama seems to tongue-in-cheek reference that cliche, at any rate.

[]:             Quote Post by Animejunkie » Tue Jan 18, 2011 4:59 am just as a side note, if this is your favorite scene i would reccomend the Raising Shinji Ikari Project as a nice read for you. Im not fully up to date and its been a while since ive had time to sit down and read but if memory serves me correct its actually an expanded version of this scenario. (please correct if im wrong). But yea just putting it out there

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Tue Jan 18, 2011 10:18 am I think the sense of that scene isn't to show us a "ridiculous perfect world/sucky excuse for reality" - look at the Dialogue following it. It's part of the "what makes you who you are" segment of the episode. All of this episode (and actually, during the entire series), Shinji has constantly been moping about how piloting EVA is the only thing he's good for. It is showed to us after Shinji's realization that "perhaps not everyone hates him", and afterwards, he realizes that there is more about him than just being an EVA-Pilot. He could have met the ppl he knows in other ways, he would still exist and be somewhat recognizable as "Shinji Ikari." He says "this is what it also could have been like." most EVA chars have very admirable traits, but also very fatal flaws, and they failed because of the latter, but we wouldn't have cared if there had never been space for them to improve/chances for them to take another road. They were NOT doomed from the start - the possibility to suceed was lost (or appeared to be lost - both versions have a good ending), and that's where the tragedy comes from. This is why we're sad. The Characters had the potential to be like they are in that sequence. This is why we're sad. This is why that scene moves us. Shinji's realization here is comparable to the one I had watching Rebuild: "Wow, Shinji was so badass in here! ...but wait, he's ALWAYS been this awesome. WOW, Asuka can actually be nice? Wait, there were actually a few glimpses of that kindness in the series... " etc. He says "If you think of that, the real world isn't that bad either". There really was this potential in the old series, there were cute little Moments... And honestly, I think Shinji's line of thought/his realization afterwards is perfectly understandable - but I havev to agree that ending the Series with that sequence wouldn't have worked - It is just a thought experiment leading up to a conclusion. Oh, and did you know that EoE was once going to have an alt-reality-sequence, too? It's still floating around on the internet, but radically different from the one in ep 26, fitting with the somewhat different emphasises/storyline/themes in EoE. But just to make it clear: NEITHER VERSION OF THE EVENTS has Shinji accepting Instrumentality. Rewatch ep. 26: "But I WANT to be here! I WANT to be me!" =/= rejection of individuality. And Rewatch 26' too, dear Bad-End-ists. "Every living thing has the ability to come back and the heart to go on living"

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Jan 18, 2011 12:14 pm I agree that that scene packs a wallop in such a bizarre, unearthly way, and I'm glad you mentioned it because it doesn't get talked about enough. In film and narratives in general, I think there's fewer things more devastating when when the saddest of things is paired with the happiest, because you always realize what was or could've been or is going to be. Most use comedy to set up drama, and NGE does this as well, but I think that invasion of light, happy comedy in the midst of the drama makes it all the more affective. Gaspar Noe's Irreversible did a similar thing, but began with tragedy before ending with the happy promise of tomorrow that we know couldn't possibly happen (because the film was told in reverse). That said, I can't agree it should've ended there because: TehDonutKing wrote: But if it ended there, the theme would be lost.

[]:             Quote Post by GoGoGengar » Thu Apr 21, 2011 5:15 pm I also love that scene. And to me it isn't sad at all... It's the breakthrough where Shinji finds hope within the depths of his mind and realizes that he can receive inner strength in order to gain confidence to recover from his depression and acknowledge the power and importance of his own existence

[]:             Quote Post by tomrule123 » Sat Apr 23, 2011 11:43 am Here's what I find interesting: If the second impact never occurred, would Asuka end up staying in Japan and would Rei exist? In NGE (and Rebuild and the Manga), Asuka was assigned to go to Japan for using the EVA 02 and Rei was created after Yui's death. Since Yui's still alive in the happy life, would Rei still be alive? Probably not. Now, if Rei became Gendo and Yui's daughter, that would make sense, since Rei's genes were from Yui. ... I wonder if Kaworu would exist? Yet again, he is a part of Adam, the first angel. And would Asuka still be living in Germany if she was never given a mission? Possibly... unless her mother never went insane to begin with and both her mother and father enjoy their lives with Asuka and thus would explain why Asuka lives in Japan in the happy side of Evangelion. That's just my guess, not using other sources to explain that to me... never read the "Shinji Ikari Raising Project" before making this post.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Apr 23, 2011 12:39 pm tomrule123 wrote: Here's what I find interesting: If the second impact never occurred, would Asuka end up staying in Japan and would Rei exist? In NGE (and Rebuild and the Manga), Asuka was assigned to go to Japan for using the EVA 02 and Rei was created after Yui's death. Since Yui's still alive in the happy life, would Rei still be alive? Probably not. Now, if Rei became Gendo and Yui's daughter, that would make sense, since Rei's genes were from Yui. ... .

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Sat Apr 23, 2011 3:25 pm @tomrule123 In the spin-off manga most of these issues are hand-waved away as not being important, with Rei being some sort of distant relation, and everything set up to be the action arc sans angst, if not always sans super-robot action. Kendrix wrote: But if Rei was his real sister, that would rule her out as a love interest.

[]:             Quote Post by Kendrix » Sat Apr 23, 2011 5:30 pm Mr. Tines wrote: @tomrule123 In the spin-off manga most of these issues are hand-waved away as not being important, with Rei being some sort of distant relation, and everything set up to be the action arc sans angst, if not always sans super-robot action. A fair number of anime series from the last year or so would beg to disagree.
